name: CodexGraph
maintainer: Alibaba Tongyi Lab / ModelScope team (primary authors: Xiangyan Liu, Bo Lan, Zhiyuan Hu, Yang Liu, Zhicheng Zhang, Fei Wang, Michael Shieh, Wenmeng Zhou)
last_major_release: [uncertain] Integrated into ms-agent v1.1.0 (released 2025-07-31); no standalone version tag
activity: [uncertain] Hosted within modelscope/modelscope-agent (~3.3k stars); CodexGraph sub-directory receives sporadic maintenance; contributor count and commit frequency for the sub-app not separately tracked as of 2026-04
storage_backend: Neo4j (property graph database); Cypher query interface exposed to LLM agents
representation_primitive: code-AST / entity+edge. The repository is parsed via static analysis into a property graph where nodes represent source code symbols (MODULE, CLASS, METHOD, FUNCTION, FIELD, GLOBAL_VARIABLE) and edges encode structural relationships (CONTAINS, INHERITS, HAS_METHOD, HAS_FIELD, USES). Each node carries metadata attributes (name, file_path, class, code, signature). No vector embeddings are used for primary retrieval — the agent issues Cypher graph queries instead.
api_shape: Python library integrated into ModelScope-Agent framework. No first-party MCP server. Exposed via five application scenarios built on top of modelscope-agent. CLI and programmatic Python API available through the agent framework.
hosting: Self-host only. No managed cloud or SaaS offering.
llm_dependency: Both ingest and retrieval. Ingest: a static-analysis pipeline builds the graph without LLM (pure AST extraction). Retrieval: a dual-LLM-agent design — a primary reasoning agent decomposes tasks and issues natural-language queries; a translation agent converts those to executable Cypher. Evaluated with GPT-4o, DeepSeek-Coder-V2, and Qwen2-72b-Instruct.
schema_extensibility: fixed (for current Python extractor). Schema is prescribed: six node types and five edge types are hard-coded by the static analysis pipeline. The paper notes the schema 'admits future extensions to polyglot or federated codebases' but no YAML-config or LLM-learned schema extension is implemented in the current release.
schema_richness: 6 node types: MODULE, CLASS, METHOD, FUNCTION, FIELD, GLOBAL_VARIABLE. 5 edge types: CONTAINS, INHERITS, HAS_METHOD, HAS_FIELD, USES. Multi-label: not described. Multi-graph: single unified property graph. Compared to Gimle §5 target (20+ entities / 15+ edges): CodexGraph covers only code-structural relations; it does not model decisions, iterations, issues, API endpoints, UI components, or bi-temporal metadata. The schema is deep on code structure but narrow in scope.
temporal_model: none. No temporal metadata (valid_from/to, recorded_at, event-log) on nodes or edges. The graph is a static snapshot rebuilt from the current codebase state. No bi-temporal or incremental update model described.
forgetting_policy: none. The graph is fully rebuilt from source on each ingest run. No eviction, deduplication, or soft-delete mechanism.
ingest_latency_class: batch-offline. Static analysis traverses the entire repository to build the full graph before any query is possible. Latency scales with repo size (minutes for large repos).
retrieval_modes: graph-traversal exclusively. The LLM translation agent generates Cypher queries that traverse the Neo4j property graph. No vector/embedding search, no BM25 full-text search. Complex composite queries (e.g., 'find all classes in module X that INHERITS from Y and contain method Z') are expressible via multi-hop Cypher.
retrieval_agenticity: multi-hop with dual-agent pipeline. The primary agent issues iterative graph queries, receives result tuples, and can refine follow-up queries until an output condition is met. This constitutes a dynamic, multi-step agentic retrieval loop rather than a single static lookup.
retrieval_budget_control: no. No explicit per-query token/hop/edge budget parameters are exposed. Query depth is implicitly constrained by Cypher complexity and the LLM's generation limits.
multi_project_namespace: none in current implementation. CodexGraph indexes one repository at a time; there is no built-in multi-project or federated namespace. Cross-project queries are not supported.
multi_user_access: none. No RBAC, per-agent namespace, or provenance-aware sharing. Single-user/single-agent model.
embedder_options: none. CodexGraph does not use embeddings. Retrieval is entirely graph-query-based via Cypher. No embedding adapters or vector index.
llm_client_options: OpenAI API (GPT-4o default in experiments), DeepSeek-Coder-V2, Qwen2-72b-Instruct evaluated. Plugged in via ModelScope-Agent's LLM abstraction layer. LLM cannot be disabled — it is required for both query translation and reasoning.
cost_privacy: Token cost at ingest: near-zero (static analysis only, no LLM calls during graph construction). Token cost at retrieval: moderate — each query invokes two LLM calls (primary agent + translation agent), plus iterative refinement steps. Total per-task cost depends on task complexity and number of query iterations. Data stays on-premise if self-hosted with local models; otherwise query text and code snippets are sent to the configured LLM provider API.
code_artifact_linkage: Symbol (function/class) level — first-class. File-path level — first-class (file_path attribute on all nodes). Line-level defs+refs — not exposed (no line numbers on edges). AST-node level — not exposed as queryable nodes. Issue-PR-to-symbol chain — not supported. Ranked: (1) symbol-level (function/class/method/field), (2) file-path, (3) module-level import resolution via USES edges.
code_analysis_features: First-party: call graph (via HAS_METHOD + USES edges), import resolution (USES edges across modules), inheritance graph (INHERITS edges), symbol index (all node types queryable by name/signature). Not first-party / absent: diff-awareness (no incremental update), type-aware analysis (types not stored as graph attributes), cross-language support (Python-only in current release), dependency graph across packages (only intra-repo).
memory_os_layer: plaintext (external memory). Operates entirely at the external-storage tier — the graph database stores code structure as explicit nodes and edges outside model weights. No parametric (weight tuning) or activation (KV-cache) tier. Cross-type migration not supported.
self_improvement_mechanism: none. The graph is rebuilt from source code; no feedback loop from execution results, no LLM-reflection, no online alias learning.
provenance_standard: none. No audit trail, W3C-PROV, or OpenTelemetry traces emitted. Query logs available only at the database level (standard Neo4j logging).
benchmark_results: Evaluated on three benchmarks (paper arXiv 2408.03910v2, NAACL 2025): (1) CrossCodeEval Lite (Python), GPT-4o backbone: EM=27.9%, ES=67.98% — surpasses BM25 and embedding baselines. (2) SWE-bench (Lite): Pass@1=22.96% — on par with AutoCodeRover. (3) EvoCodeBench: Pass@1=36.02%, Recall@1=11.87% — exceeds other toolkits evaluated. No LongMemEval, LoCoMo, HotpotQA, or MultiHop results reported (different domain).
strengths: 1. Precision over recall: graph-traversal Cypher queries eliminate the fuzzy-match false-positives endemic to embedding-based retrieval, enabling exact structural lookups ('all classes inheriting from X in module Y'). 2. Composable multi-hop reasoning: the dual-agent pipeline can issue iterative Cypher queries, supporting complex cross-file reasoning that single-shot RAG cannot. 3. Zero-embedding-cost ingest: the static-analysis pipeline builds a rich structural index with no LLM or embedding calls, making graph construction fast and cost-free for large repos.
weaknesses: 1. Python-only: the static analysis extractor supports only Python repositories; multi-language codebases (TypeScript, Kotlin, etc.) are unsupported in current release. 2. No temporal / incremental updates: the graph must be fully rebuilt on every code change; no diff-aware patching or bi-temporal history. 3. Schema too narrow for Gimle §5: covers only code-structural relations — no support for decisions, iterations, issues, API endpoints, UI components, or any product/process domain concepts required by Gimle's target schema.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas. CodexGraph is not adoptable whole — it covers only code-structure and lacks temporal modeling, multi-language support, and the broad entity vocabulary Gimle §5 targets. Specific ideas to steal: (1) Dual-agent Cypher query pattern — separate the 'reasoning agent' (decomposes intent into NL sub-queries) from the 'translation agent' (converts NL sub-queries to graph queries). Gimle can adopt this pattern for its Graphiti-backed store, routing agent queries through a Cypher/GQL translation layer rather than embedding-only lookups. (2) Property graph schema for code artifacts — CodexGraph's node+edge schema maps cleanly onto Gimle §5's Symbol, File, Module entities. The HAS_METHOD, INHERITS, CONTAINS, USES edge types can be directly adopted or extended with Gimle's issue-PR-to-symbol-chain requirement. Example schema snippet directly reusable: MODULE -[:CONTAINS]-> CLASS -[:HAS_METHOD]-> FUNCTION, with file_path, signature, code attributes on FUNCTION nodes. (3) Two-phase static ingest — shallow intra-file AST indexing followed by cross-file import resolution is the correct order for building Gimle's symbol graph without false edges.
